items.question_text: Under 9-4-1, when information is received that an aircraft plans to dump fuel, which of the following must the controller determine?
items.explanation: Per 9-4-1, the controller must determine the route and altitude the aircraft will fly AND the weather conditions in which the operation will be conducted. All three elements are required.

choices.text: Only the route, because altitude can be assigned later and weather is irrelevant to fuel dumping.
The route and altitude it will fly, and the weather conditions in which the operation will be conducted.
Only the altitude and fuel quantity remaining, because route is presumed from the flight plan.
The route, altitude, weather conditions, and the pilot's estimate of remaining flight time after the dump.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-1, which trigger event obligates the controller to determine route, altitude, and weather conditions?
items.explanation: 9-4-1 is triggered when information is received that an aircraft plans to dump fuel. It does not require the dump to have already started.

choices.text: When the dump actually begins and fuel is observed leaving the aircraft.
When information is received that an aircraft plans to dump fuel.
Only if the fuel dump will occur in IFR conditions.
Only after the pilot declares an emergency and requests a fuel-dump clearance.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-1, an aircraft reports it plans to dump fuel in 10 minutes. The controller responds by asking only what altitude the aircraft will use. Is this sufficient?
items.explanation: No. 9-4-1 requires determination of route, altitude, AND weather conditions. Asking only for altitude omits two required pieces of information.

choices.text: Yes, because altitude is the only safety-critical element for separation purposes.
No, because the controller must also determine the route and the weather conditions.
Yes, because the route is already known from the flight plan and weather can be determined from ATIS.
No, because the controller must also determine the fuel quantity remaining and the number of persons on board.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-1, which of the three required determinations is most likely to affect whether the operation can safely proceed at all?
items.explanation: Weather conditions directly affect whether dumping can safely occur. Route and altitude are operational parameters that can be adjusted, but weather conditions (icing, turbulence, visibility) may make the operation unsafe. 9-4-1 requires all three.

choices.text: Route, because routing determines which sectors are affected.
Altitude, because altitude determines the obstacle clearance requirements under 9-4-3.
Weather conditions, because adverse weather may make the fuel-dumping operation unsafe regardless of route or altitude.
All three are equally critical because 9-4-1 does not prioritize any one over the others.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-2, an aircraft is dumping fuel because of a medical emergency on board. May the controller request it to fly a different route?
items.explanation: No. 9-4-2 states 'Except when it is dumping fuel for emergency reasons.' A medical emergency is an emergency reason, so the aircraft may NOT be requested to fly a different route.

choices.text: Yes, because the rule allows route requests in all fuel-dumping situations.
Yes, but only if the aircraft is IFR.
No, because the aircraft is dumping fuel for emergency reasons.
No, because fuel-dumping aircraft may never be rerouted regardless of reason.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-2, a VFR aircraft is dumping fuel to reduce landing weight (non-emergency). The controller wants to route it away from a populated area. Is this permitted?
items.explanation: Yes. 9-4-2 permits requesting a different route when the dump is NOT for emergency reasons, and the rule applies in both VFR and IFR conditions.

choices.text: No, because VFR aircraft are not covered by the rule.
No, because any fuel dump is treated as an emergency under 9-4-2.
Yes, because the dump is not for emergency reasons and the rule applies in either VFR or IFR conditions.
Yes, but only if the aircraft first transitions to IFR.

items.question_text: Fill in the blank: Under 9-4-2, except when it is dumping fuel for ________ reasons, an aircraft in either VFR or IFR conditions may be requested to fly a different route.
items.explanation: The key triggering condition is 'emergency.' 9-4-2's exception applies only when the fuel dump is for emergency reasons.

choices.text: emergency
weight-reduction
operational
maintenance

items.question_text: Under 9-4-2, a controller tells a fuel-dumping aircraft 'Fly heading 270 for traffic.' The dump is not for emergency reasons. Is this an authorized use of the rule?
items.explanation: Yes. 9-4-2 authorizes requesting a different route for non-emergency fuel dumps. A heading change constitutes a route modification. The rule applies regardless of whether the aircraft is VFR or IFR.

choices.text: No, because 9-4-2 only authorizes complete route amendments, not simple heading changes.
No, because heading changes require emergency authority under 9-4-2.
Yes, because a heading change is a route modification authorized for non-emergency fuel dumps under 9-4-2.
Yes, but only if the aircraft is operating IFR.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-3, if an aircraft is dumping fuel in IFR conditions, what is the minimum altitude above the highest obstacle within 5 miles of the route or pattern being flown?
items.explanation: Per 9-4-3, the altitude assigned must be at least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles of the route or pattern being flown.

choices.text: At least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles.
At least 1,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles.
Exactly 2,000 feet above the airport elevation.
2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 10 miles of the dump pattern.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-3, does the IFR fuel-dumping altitude requirement apply to VFR fuel-dumping operations?
items.explanation: No. 9-4-3 explicitly states 'If an aircraft is dumping fuel in IFR conditions.' VFR fuel-dumping altitude assignments are not governed by this paragraph.

choices.text: Yes, because 9-4-3 applies to all fuel-dumping operations regardless of flight conditions.
No, because 9-4-3 applies only to fuel-dumping operations conducted in IFR conditions.
Yes, but with the obstacle radius reduced to 3 miles for VFR operations.
No, because VFR fuel-dumping operations use a 1,000-foot obstacle clearance standard instead.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-3, an aircraft dumping fuel IFR is flying a pattern. The highest obstacle within 5 miles of that pattern is a 3,200-foot tower. What is the minimum assignable altitude?
items.explanation: 9-4-3 requires at least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle. 3,200 + 2,000 = 5,200 feet MSL. The controller may assign higher, but 5,200 feet is the minimum.

choices.text: 3,200 feet MSL, because the aircraft is above the highest obstacle.
5,200 feet MSL.
4,200 feet MSL, using standard IFR obstacle clearance.
2,000 feet MSL, because 9-4-3 sets a flat 2,000-foot minimum altitude for all fuel-dumping operations.

items.question_text: Fill in the blank: Under 9-4-3, if an aircraft is dumping fuel in IFR conditions, assign an altitude at least ______ feet above the highest obstacle within ______ miles of the route or pattern being flown.
items.explanation: The two critical numeric thresholds are 2,000 feet and 5 miles. Both must be exact — these are not general values.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-4, which of the following is NOT a valid method for separating known IFR aircraft from an aircraft dumping fuel?
items.explanation: 9-4-4a lists four valid methods: 1,000 feet above (or greater per 4-5-1), 2,000 feet below, 5 miles radar, and 5 miles laterally. 3 miles is not listed.

choices.text: 5 miles laterally.
3 miles radar.
2,000 feet below.
1,000 feet above it, or in accordance with 4-5-1 if greater.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-4, when separating IFR aircraft ABOVE the aircraft dumping fuel, which paragraph governs if the standard vertical separation would be greater than 1,000 feet?
items.explanation: 9-4-4a1 states '1,000 feet above it; or in accordance with paragraph 4-5-1, Vertical Separation Minima, whichever is greater.' The controller must apply the larger of the two values.

choices.text: Paragraph 4-5-1, Vertical Separation Minima.
Paragraph 5-6-1, Application.
Paragraph 9-4-3, Altitude Assignment.
No other paragraph applies; 1,000 feet is always sufficient for above separation.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-4, what is the separation requirement for VFR radar-identified aircraft from the aircraft dumping fuel?
items.explanation: Per 9-4-4b, VFR radar-identified aircraft must be separated by 5 miles and in accordance with paragraph 5-6-1, Application.

choices.text: 1,000 feet above and 2,000 feet below, same as IFR aircraft.
5 miles and in accordance with paragraph 5-6-1.
3 miles if the aircraft has an operating transponder.
Traffic advisories only, because VFR aircraft are not subject to fuel-dump separation standards.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-4, an aircraft is dumping fuel at FL 200. An IFR aircraft at FL 210 is directly above it. Is the separation sufficient under 9-4-4?
items.explanation: 9-4-4a1 requires 1,000 feet above OR in accordance with 4-5-1, WHICHEVER IS GREATER. At FL 200/FL 210, 4-5-1 requires 1,000 feet above FL 290 and 2,000 feet at/above FL 290 for non-RVSM. Here at FL 200 (below FL 290), 4-5-1 requires 1,000 feet. So 1,000 feet is both the 9-4-4 minimum and the 4-5-1 minimum. FL 210 is exactly 1,000 feet above FL 200 — sufficient.

choices.text: Yes, because 1,000 feet above meets both the 9-4-4 minimum and the 4-5-1 standard at this altitude.
No, because 9-4-4 always requires at least 2,000 feet above for fuel-dumping separation.
No, because 4-5-1 requires 2,000 feet at FL 200 and above.
Yes, but only if RVSM separation is applied.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-4, an aircraft is dumping fuel. An IFR aircraft is 2,000 feet directly below it and 4 miles laterally from the dump track. Is this separation sufficient?
items.explanation: The aircraft is using two forms of separation that do not need to be combined. The vertical separation of 2,000 feet below meets 9-4-4a2 independently. The 4 miles lateral does not meet the 5 miles lateral requirement of 9-4-4a4, but the 2,000-foot-below vertical separation is sufficient on its own.

choices.text: No, because lateral separation requires 5 miles.
Yes, because 2,000 feet below independently satisfies 9-4-4a2 regardless of lateral distance.
No, because vertical and lateral minima must both be met simultaneously.
Yes, because 4 miles laterally is close enough to 5 miles to be acceptable.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-5, when you are in contact with an aircraft that starts dumping fuel, what is your first action?
items.explanation: Per 9-4-5a, inform other controllers and facilities which might be concerned. This is the first step before the broadcast cycle begins.

choices.text: Broadcast a fuel-dumping advisory immediately on the frequency.
Inform other controllers and facilities which might be concerned.
Vector all other aircraft at least 5 miles away from the dumping aircraft.
Ask the pilot how long the dump will take.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-5, at what interval must concerned facilities broadcast the fuel-dumping advisory on appropriate radio frequencies?
items.explanation: Per 9-4-5a, facilities concerned must broadcast an advisory at 3-minute intervals until the dumping stops.

choices.text: At 3-minute intervals until the dumping stops.
Once at the start and once when complete, with no intermediate broadcasts.
At 5-minute intervals until the aircraft leaves the facility's airspace.
Every minute until all traffic has acknowledged the advisory.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-5, which of the following is the correct terminating advisory phraseology?
items.explanation: Per 9-4-5b phraseology, the terminating advisory is 'ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT. FUEL DUMPING OVER (location) TERMINATED.' The word 'TERMINATED' is prescribed.

choices.text: ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT. FUEL DUMPING OVER (location) COMPLETE.
ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT. FUEL DUMPING OVER (location) TERMINATED.
ALL STATIONS. FUEL DUMPING OVER (location) HAS ENDED.
ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT. FUEL DUMPING OVER (location) ENDED.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-5, what four elements does the in-progress fuel-dumping advisory broadcast contain?
items.explanation: Per 9-4-5 phraseology: 'ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT. FUEL DUMPING IN PROGRESS OVER (location) AT (altitude) BY (type aircraft) (flight direction).' The four elements are location, altitude, aircraft type, and flight direction.

choices.text: Location, altitude, aircraft type, and flight direction.
Location, altitude, estimated duration, and fuel quantity.
Location, aircraft callsign, altitude, and heading.
Location, altitude, aircraft type, and reason for dumping.

items.question_text: Under 9-4-5, who is responsible for broadcasting the fuel-dumping advisory?
items.explanation: Per 9-4-5a, 'Facilities concerned must broadcast an advisory.' This is a facility-level obligation. The controller in contact with the aircraft informs other facilities; those concerned facilities then broadcast.

choices.text: Only the controller in direct contact with the dumping aircraft.
Facilities concerned must broadcast the advisory on appropriate radio frequencies.
The pilot of the dumping aircraft, as part of the fuel-dump notification.
Only the facility supervisor, after coordinating with the center.

items.question_text: Fill in the blank: Under 9-4-5, facilities concerned must broadcast an advisory on appropriate radio frequencies at ______ intervals until the dumping stops.
items.explanation: The prescribed interval is 3 minutes. This is a specific numeric threshold that must be exact.

choices.text: 3-minute
5-minute
1-minute
10-minute

items.situation: A B767 pilot reports plans to dump fuel to reduce landing weight. The trainee immediately begins coordinating with adjacent sectors for traffic reroutes without first asking the pilot any questions.
items.question_text: What information must you obtain from the pilot before proceeding with coordination?

choices.text: The route and altitude the aircraft will fly during the dump, and the weather conditions in which the operation will be conducted.
Only the pilot's estimate of how much fuel will remain after the dump.
Only whether the pilot prefers VFR or IFR for the dumping operation.
No additional information is needed because the flight plan already provides the route and altitude.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-1, when information is received that an aircraft plans to dump fuel, determine the route and altitude it will fly and the weather conditions in which the operation will be conducted.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft is dumping fuel to meet maximum landing weight (non-emergency). Its current route passes over a populated area. A controller says you cannot change its route because the aircraft is IFR and the 7110.65 restricts fuel-dump route changes to VFR only.
items.question_text: May you request the aircraft to fly a different route?

choices.text: Yes, because 9-4-2 allows requesting a different route for non-emergency fuel dumps in either VFR or IFR conditions.
No, because 9-4-2 only authorizes route changes when the aircraft is VFR.
No, because any fuel-dumping operation is treated as an emergency under 9-4-2.
Yes, but only after the dump is complete and the aircraft is below 10,000 feet.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-2, except when dumping for emergency reasons, an aircraft in either VFR or IFR conditions may be requested to fly a different route.

items.situation: An aircraft is dumping fuel IFR and flying a pattern. The highest charted obstacle within 5 miles of the pattern is a 4,500-foot peak. The trainee assigns 5,500 feet. The aircraft's standard IFR altitude assignment per 4-5-1 would be 5,000 feet.
items.question_text: Is the trainee's altitude assignment of 5,500 feet correct under 9-4-3?

choices.text: No. 9-4-3 requires at least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles (4,500 + 2,000 = 6,500 feet MSL minimum). 5,500 feet is only 1,000 feet above the obstacle.
Yes. 5,500 feet provides the standard 1,000-foot IFR obstacle clearance, which is sufficient.
Yes. 5,500 feet is 1,000 feet above the peak, and 9-4-3 only requires 1,000 feet of obstacle clearance.
No. 9-4-3 requires exactly 2,000 feet MSL above ground level, not above the highest obstacle.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-3, IFR fuel-dumping aircraft must be assigned at least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles of the route or pattern. 4,500 + 2,000 = 6,500 feet minimum.

items.situation: An aircraft is dumping fuel at FL 310 (above FL 290). An IFR aircraft is at FL 320, 1,000 feet directly above the dumping aircraft. A trainee says the separation is fine because 9-4-4a1 only requires 1,000 feet above.
items.question_text: Is 1,000 feet above sufficient separation in this case?

choices.text: No, because above FL 290, 4-5-1 requires 2,000 feet vertical separation (non-RVSM), and 9-4-4a1 requires the greater of 1,000 feet or 4-5-1. So 2,000 feet is needed.
Yes, because 9-4-4a1 always uses a flat 1,000-foot above standard for fuel-dumping separation.
Yes, because at FL 310, RVSM reduces the requirement to 1,000 feet above FL 290.
No, because 9-4-4a1 requires 2,000 feet above regardless of altitude.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-4a1, separation above the dumping aircraft is '1,000 feet above it; or in accordance with paragraph 4-5-1, Vertical Separation Minima, whichever is greater.' Above FL 290, 4-5-1 requires 2,000 feet (non-RVSM). The greater value (2,000 feet) applies.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft on flight following (radar-identified) is 4 miles from an aircraft actively dumping fuel. The trainee says advisory-only service is sufficient because the VFR aircraft is not IFR.
items.question_text: Is 4 miles sufficient separation for this VFR radar-identified aircraft?

choices.text: No. 9-4-4b requires 5 miles for VFR radar-identified aircraft from the aircraft dumping fuel, and in accordance with 5-6-1.
Yes, because VFR aircraft only need traffic advisories regardless of distance from the dumping aircraft.
Yes, because 4 miles is close enough and the pilot can see and avoid.
No, because VFR aircraft must be separated by 10 miles from fuel-dumping operations.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-4b, VFR radar-identified aircraft must be separated by 5 miles and in accordance with paragraph 5-6-1 from the aircraft dumping fuel.

items.situation: You are working an aircraft that begins dumping fuel over the BUCKEYE VOR at 8,000 feet, heading east. A trainee says to broadcast the advisory once and then wait for pilot reports before broadcasting again.
items.question_text: How should the fuel-dumping advisory be handled?

choices.text: Inform other controllers and facilities that might be concerned. The concerned facilities must then broadcast 'ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT. FUEL DUMPING IN PROGRESS OVER BUCKEYE AT 8,000 BY (type) EASTBOUND' at 3-minute intervals until the dumping stops, then broadcast the terminating advisory.
Broadcast one advisory and wait for further pilot reports before taking additional action.
Only broadcast the terminating advisory when the dump ends; in-progress advisories are optional.
Notify the supervisor and let them determine the broadcast schedule.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-5, inform other controllers/facilities, broadcast advisories at 3-minute intervals until the dumping stops, then broadcast the terminating advisory.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.question_text: Identify the incorrect word in the broadcast: ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT. FUEL DUMPING OVER BUCKEYE COMPLETED.
items.original_phrase: ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT. FUEL DUMPING OVER BUCKEYE TERMINATED.
items.display_text: ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT FUEL DUMPING OVER BUCKEYE COMPLETED
items.explanation: Per 9-4-5b, the prescribed terminating advisory word is 'TERMINATED,' not 'COMPLETED.'
